narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 55.3% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is just over the threshold we use to approve admissions. This prediction is based on your profile as a female student from a White background planning to attend full-time, with a middle-class family income, an LSAT score of 39, an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, and school decile rankings of 2nd in both your first and third years. Compared to typical students, your LSAT stands out as stronger than average, while your early and later school deciles are notably lower than the usual 5th or 6th place, and your GPA is a touch below the standard 3.21. The model's decision was shaped most strongly by your LSAT score, which surprisingly nudged the prediction toward a higher risk of failure in your specific case. Right behind that, your low first-year decile had a major pull in the same direction, followed closely by your third-year decile doing the same. Your family income level ranked next in influence, also contributing toward the failure risk, with your undergraduate GPA not far after exerting a similar push. The rest—your racial background, gender, and full-time status—played smaller roles, all leaning slightly toward increased risk but with less weight. In essence, no single strength fully offset the combined drag from your academic deciles and GPA relative to what the model sees in successful bar passers. To boost your future chances, focus on realistic steps like targeted prep to strengthen bar exam skills, perhaps building on your solid LSAT foundation through practice tests or tutoring. You might also consider ways to demonstrate academic improvement, such as excelling in any upcoming coursework or retaking the LSAT if it could push even higher. With your full-time commitment and background aligning with most admitted students, addressing those early grade deciles' implications through proven study strategies could tip the scales next time. Hang in there—this is just one data point, and many students turn predictions around with focused effort.
